review_text: This is one of the best movies I have ever watched. This move inspired be to get a drumset and start drumming, now I wold say I'm pretty good. This movie is one of the best emotional dramas ever. The acting of some of the best acting I have ever seen in a movie. I wish I would erase my memory and watch this for the first time again. This movie is quite my tempo.

Whiplash touches upon the inherent drive for greatness that many creatives experience and its follies when those desires are abused and pushed to their limit. Showcasing a journey that's disgusting and hard to look away from, as the protagonist's dreams, background, and insecurities are used against him until he is completely unraveled. This is supplemented by an outstanding performance of music, acting, and cinematography that makes this film a masterwork in depicting the darker side of creative ambition. Truly a 10/10 unforgettable film that will leave audiences shocked for years to come.
This movie was not rushing or dragging. It was a perfect mix of emotional, fun, brutal, rebellious, and awsome. Amazing plot, great characters. My tempo.

To be honest, I watched an hour and 40 minutes of banging on drums.. pretty boring. I get the theme of secretive for art; however, I really couldn’t care less that the main guy gets a smile at the end. Maybe condense into 40 minutes… not an hour and 40.
A film that deliberately provokes frustration, driven by J.K. Simmons’ commanding and intense performance. His work elevates the tension and anchors the film’s exploration of obsession and ambition.
It was great start to finish.

Follows the intense passion of a young man (Miles Teller) who is willing to do anything it takes to be successful even at the expense of family, romantic interests, and his own physical/mental well-being. Jk Simmons is cast perfectly as he plays a menacing music instructor, giving viewers a window into the high stress world at a top music school.
Had to adjust my seat height for this one yo
Cativante, filme que você não sente a hora passar, a obsessão pela perfeição faz os dois personagens perderem a cabeça e que atuação dos dois mas o JK Simmons mereceu demais o Oscar que ganhou 9.0
this movie is a masterclass in acting and music

I have known this film for more than ten years. The first time I watched it continuously from beginning to end. Andrew, a talented young drummer who want to become one of the greatest musicians. He practices relentlessly, even when his hands are bleeding. He breaks up with his girlfriend so that he can devote more time to training, and he endures constant humiliation and pressure from his instructor, Fletcher, who is extremely strict and often ruthless. He believes that he can survive under such intense pressure. Even after being involved in a serious car accident, he still insists on returning to the stage to continue the performance. Whether such brutal training is truly necessary for success. For most people, this level of pressure would be overwhelming and destructive. Andrew is already incredibly brave to take control of the music on stage when the film ends abruptly.

I went into this movie bored, with nothing to do, and clicked on it on Netflix fully expecting another over-hyped, overrated film. I’ll admit—I already had a negative bias going in. What followed completely blindsided me. This is, without exaggeration, one of the best movies I have ever seen.

The last fifteen minutes of Whiplash feel like cinema at its absolute limit. Not triumph, not defeat, but the raw culmination of human aspiration. A moment where talent, suffering, and willpower converge and nothing else exists.
masterpiece. en shock
Teller finally turned into what Fletcher thinks of perfection, after abusing him in every way possible, Fletcher created a monster, good or bad.

J.K. Simmons AND Miles Teller puts on their best performances of their carriers. As someone who loves jazz this is the perfect movie. This is in my top 5 favorite movies ever. Yes it's that good just watch it.
The suspense and intensity keeps you hooked until the credits roll and you’ll be thinking about the ending long after it’s over!
Whiplash is an amazing movie. Of course it has it's own issues for other ratings, this is still a great story. It's solidified on my list of recommendations and I don't think that'll ever change.

Brilliant script with stellar performances neither of which shy away from the uncomfortable but not at the risk of alienating it's audience yet it never condescends. The film examines the sacrifice, discipline, stoicism, and masochism required to rise to the level of "great art" with a driving pace. Chazelle artfully uses lighting making it claustrophobic at times, illuminating at others and seductive always, close-ups that can be intimidating then vulnerable all of which is undergirded by an appropriately propulsive musical score. Similar to Newton's law of motion, neither principle actor or script becomes inert; proving more than engaging but absolutely magnetic. A complete tour de force from Simmons and Teller each performance is nuanced but deftly commanding making this film decisively one of the most memorable and magnificent character studies of cinema.
Very intense. Loved it.

Fourth viewing of this now and still one of my favourite movies of all time, top 20 easily. Outstanding throughout, JK Simmons with one the best supporting actor performances ever, Miles Teller is brilliant too and with one of the best endings in cinema. Watching these two guys compete, has this rivalry that in a sense for better or for worse bring the best out in Neiman, no matter how much it takes it toll on the pair of them to achieve greatness. Toxic boss off the scale
From the cinematography to music. Actors and script. This movie is perfect.

My teenager wanted me to watch this with him. I didn’t think I’d like it. However, It was truly one of the best movies. Even if you’ve never made it even close to these musicians level , most of us have had something we’ve done that stretched us to a point we didn’t know if we’d accomplish and most of us have had ,"teachers," that teach by cruelty and complete and utter self interest, and it feels like they are just one more thing to get through . Worse they often take credit for you succeeding at all after - thinking they beat you into it , even if they try to sabotage you occasionally. Most can relate to the cruel teacher and wanting something so bad and feeling consumed by that pursuit. Some might think the teacher was needed in the process , some might think this kid would have succeeded anyway, without the PTSD afterwards. It’s a good discussion movie. Much more real than the selfless , hard but loving coach we often see in these types of films.

I love this movie, if time travel was invented I would travel back to my younger self and thank for watching it. It's crazy how also you can see a lot of great characters and relationship build in such a great "tempo" movie

My favorite movie of all time. Nothing like it

i cannot legally give it less than 5 stars but i have criticisms about the way the car crash was handled afterwards in the movie, it felt as though there were lasting consequences that survived after the initial purpose for all events in the film with exception to that one outside of it’s purpose. realism isn’t very important to me in films like this and i understand it’s purpose metaphorically and literally and i believe it is genius. but it isn’t impossible to utilize it later into the story or to weave into conversation or use as a means to drive further tension between andrew and his father or maybe to emphasize neiman’s struggle. i hope i’ve expressed this point well enough to be understood. other than this one insanely small nitpick, this was an incredible movie. 10/10. genius acting. amazing story. beautiful ending.

I rarely give 5 stars, but this movie deserves it. I watched this in college for the first time, and while it has incredibly vulgar language, the strength of the profanity matches the impact factor of this film. It left me contemplative of what I would be willing to do to become great. The movie is so much less about jazz as it is about sacrificing everything to become “one of the greats”. I’m pretty sure the director also did “La La Land,” which had a very similar theme. Loved the movie, loved the message, loved the cinematography, loved the acting and script, and I loved how much emotion this movie made me feel.

I played drums in my grade school band for nine years, and I played in the jazz band for the last two years. That kind of music is much more difficult than people probably think, and this film showed a bit of that. The film had such a simple premise, and like, there was nothing that I could point out that was wrong with this film. Everything just worked. Even for such a simple thing, they still threw in a few surprises that made things feel full circle. And man, Miles Teller and J.K. Simmons had insane chemistry here, not just through words, but their actions.

It took me too long to discover this masterpiece
Whiplash is pure, unfiltered cinema — a breathtaking exploration of ambition, pain, and perfection. If you love music and powerful drama, this film will hit every note.

As a person who started playing trumpet in elementary school and continued in band through high school, this movie triggers me. While the band directors I encountered and studied under over the years never quite reached the intensity and manipulative nature that J.K. Simmons brings to the role of “ Terence Fletcher”, his portrayal isn’t as exaggerated as you might think. The great debate that serves as the centerpiece for this film is one that, in my opinion, doesn’t really have a definitive answer: What does it take to make someone great? “Andrew Neiman”, the ambitious young drummer played by Miles Teller, makes it known early on that his purpose in life is to be one of the best musicians of all time. In his eyes, the only way to do that is to sacrifice everything, which includes denying himself of authentic human connections, because they are merely distractions on his path to greatness. Neiman finds himself hand-picked to play in the prestigious studio ensemble by Fletcher at the fictional Shaffer Conservatory in New York City. At first, I honestly felt bad for the torturous behavior he endured under the tutelage of his demanding conductor. Fletcher justifies using deception and misguided trust because he believes his methods are what propel a person into uncovering where their true talent lies. Slowly, we watch Neiman evolve from a bright-eyed kid with an almost romanticized version of what it takes to be great into someone who is forced to realize the harsh reality of what greatness truly requires. While I abhor the cruelty that Fletcher employs on his students, in the case of Neiman, the end justified the means. J.K. Simmons deserved the Oscar he received for playing Fletcher. Watching his personality ebb and flow as he manipulates his students into getting what he wants from them borders on being a character study in evil. I honestly believe that some people need a push to fully grasp the magnitude of their underlying talent. However, after watching this movie three times, I’m still not sure there is a definitive answer as to how far a person needs to be pushed to reach that point. Each time I’ve watched this, I’ve walked away with a different perspective. I’m not sure I’ve found the correct one, but this movie continues to challenge my beliefs with each subsequent viewing. And to me, that’s what makes this a fantastic piece of filmmaking and art.

A different kind of horror movie. Yes, that's right, I said horror movie. It's a horror in the sense that it's horrific to see the lengths one will go through for desperate approval and validation of an abuser.
Unforgettable. The acting, the music, the story. It belongs with the greatest movies of all times! Watch it!

Whiplash is a drama movie directed by Damien Chazelle. It tells the story of a young drummer named Andrew Neyman who wants to become one of the best jazz drummers in the world. He studies at a famous music school where he meets a very strict teacher, Terence Fletcher. He is known for being hard on his students. When I first saw Whiplash, I thought it would be just a normal movie about music. But after a few minutes, I understood that it was something different. It is not a happy movie. It is full of tension, shouting, and emotions. Every scene feels real and strong. I could almost feel the stress that Andrew was going through. Andrew practices every day and wants to be perfect. He thinks that if he works hard enough, he can be great. Fletcher, his teacher, pushes him to his limits. Sometimes it feels like Fletcher is not a teacher but a commander. He yells, throws chairs, and insults students. It is hard to watch, but it also shows how much pressure people face when they want to be the best. The actors in this movie are amazing. Miles Teller, who plays Andrew, really looks like a real drummer. You can see how much pain and passion he has. J.K. Simmons, who plays Fletcher, is very scary but also very interesting. He shows that sometimes people hurt others because they think it helps them grow. J.K. Simmons even won an Oscar for this role. The movie made me think a lot. It asks an important question: how far should we go to reach our dreams? Is it okay to lose everything just to be perfect? Whiplash doesn’t give an answer. It just shows both sides, the success and the pain. The last scene is my favorite part. Andrew plays an amazing drum solo. It feels like he is finally free. He is not trying to impress Fletcher anymore. He is playing for himself. That ending gave me goosebumps. In my opinion, Whiplash is one of the best movies I have ever seen. It is not only about music. It is about dreams, hard work, and what it costs to be great. I would recommend it to everyone who wants to achieve something big in life.
